items.q: Why does atomic radius increase down a group?
items.correct: Each successive element has an additional electron shell, increasing the distance of outer electrons from the nucleus.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger down a group because they have more protons attracting more matter.
items.random_wrong: Atoms get bigger down a group because nuclei swell when they have more neutrons.
items.why: Students attribute size to mass/protons (a 'more stuff = bigger' intuition) rather than to the number of shells.

items.q: Why does first ionisation energy generally increase across a period?
items.correct: Nuclear charge increases while shielding stays roughly constant, so electrons are held more tightly.
items.misconception: Atoms get bigger across a period, making electrons easier to add but harder to remove.
items.random_wrong: Atoms across a period have more shells, pulling electrons closer.
items.why: Students confuse the direction of the atomic-radius trend across a period (it actually decreases) and use wrong size reasoning.

items.q: Why is the 4s orbital filled before the 3d orbital?
items.correct: Because the 4s orbital has a lower energy than 3d when both are empty, so electrons fill it first.
items.misconception: Because the 4s shell is closer to the nucleus than the 3d shell.
items.random_wrong: Because d orbitals can only fill after p orbitals in the next shell.
items.why: Students assume shell number directly correlates with distance/energy ordering, so 3-anything must come before 4-anything — but energy ordering crosses over at 4s/3d.
Correct answer: Si. “Na” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Why does Al have lower 1st IE than Mg?
items.misconception: Al has more shielding from core
items.why: It's 3p electron easier to remove than 3s. “Al has more shielding from core” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Number of valence electrons in Cl?
items.why: Exact answer: 7. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
